history.author: Hassam Nasir
history.titleZh: 新型‘GeForge’与‘GDDRHammer’攻击可通过英伟达GPU内存完全渗透系统——GPU中的Rowhammer攻击强制受保护显存区域发生比特翻转以获取读写权限
history.descriptionZh:
去年，我们报道了首例针对GPU的Rowhammer攻击（名为“GPUHammer”），证实了显存（VRAM）同样存在比特翻转漏洞。当时的研究显示，单个比特翻转就能让RTX A6000上运行的AI模型准确率从80%暴跌至0.1%。此后，又出现了两种新的Rowhammer攻击方式——“GDDRHammer”和“GeForge”（相关漏洞信息发布于gddr.fail网站）。它们不仅能攻击GPU，还能通过GPU获取CPU的根权限，进而威胁整个系统安全。

这两种攻击原理相似且目标一致，但首先需要理解Rowhammer的本质。该漏洞于2014年被发现：通过高频访问内存的特定行，会因电气干扰导致相邻行存储单元发生电荷泄漏，从而引发比特翻转（0变1或1变0）。一旦发生翻转，攻击者便可能利用内存权限漏洞提升访问权限，最终危及CPU。传统Rowhammer攻击主要针对CPU使用的DDR系列内存（如DDR3/DDR4/DDR5），而直到2025年底，研究人员才首次在配备GDDR6显存的RTX A6000上实现了对GPU的Rowhammer攻击。

新一代攻击在核心机制上有了显著进化：
1. **GDDRHammer** 采用了更智能的“内存按摩”技术：通过精确操控数据布局，将攻击目标引导至GPU驱动程序未设防的内存区域，从而实现对内存库更高效的控制（单内存库可引发129次翻转）。该攻击通过破坏GPU页表——即GPU用于映射显存物理地址的关键数据结构——打破内存隔离屏障。这相当于诱骗GPU自行跨越程序间的内存边界，最终使攻击者获得对显存的完全读写控制权。

2. **GeForge** 在GDDRHammer基础上更进一步：它直接针对更底层的GPU页目录发起攻击。页目录是页表的索引，破坏该结构意味着在更深层次上瓦解显存安全。该攻击甚至在BIOS禁用IOMMU（输入输出内存管理单元）时依然有效。实验数据显示，在RTX 3060上可诱发1,171次比特翻转，在RTX A6000上也可实现202次翻转。研究人员指出：“攻击者可通过修改GPU页表，将其指向CPU内存，从而获得对CPU全部内存的读写权限，彻底攻陷整台机器。”

**影响范围与防御措施**：
- 目前确认受影响的显存类型主要为GDDR6，测试涉及的25款GPU大多存在漏洞。更新代的GDDR6X与GDDR7显存尚未被攻破。
- 英伟达建议启用ECC（错误校验纠正）功能缓解攻击，但这会降低可用显存容量、引入性能开销，且并非所有GPU都支持该功能。
- 更有效的防护是启用BIOS中的IOMMU。该单元可为GPU等外设设备设置严格的系统内存访问边界，阻止其通过损坏的页表越界访问CPU内存（相当于将GPU置于“沙箱”中）。但默认情况下IOMMU常处于关闭状态以避免兼容性问题，且若配置不当仍可能被绕过。

**风险场景与局限**：
Rowhammer攻击仍需攻击者先在目标系统上运行恶意代码才能启动攻击过程，因此相较于远程攻击，普通用户面临的风险较低。但在共享计算环境（如运行AI任务的GPU集群）中，风险会显著升高。研究人员强调，启用IOMMU能有效阻断此类漏洞，建议高危环境用户评估开启该功能的必要性。

（注：本文技术细节基于Ars Technica报道及相关学术论文，完整研究可访问gddr.fail获取。）

history.title: Intel's potent 18-core Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus CPU lands at retail for under $200
history.description: Intel's chip matches similarly-priced AMD parts in gaming and mercilessly wallops them in multi-threaded work.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 5 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. Contact me with news and offers from other Future brands Receive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors By submitting your information you agree to the Terms & Conditions and Privacy Policy and are aged 16 or over. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter Casual hardware enthusiasts and folks on the periphery of the hobby may have heard some buzz around Intel's new Arrow Lake Refresh processors, and now the iGPU-less Intel Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus is available for a cool $199, while the full-featured Intel Core Ultra 5 250K Plus is $215. Look, it's not just that the Core Ultra 200S "Plus" processors are that much better than the original recipe (and they are better); rather, it's simply that Arrow Lake was never that bad to begin with. Now that Intel has cut pricing on its processors, the Core Ultra 7 270K Plus and Core Ultra 5 250K Plus are looking downright attractive, especially now that the Core Ultra 5 250K Plus is down to $199 at Newegg.Technically, that's still over the MSR—err, sorry, Recommended Customer Price of $184, but it's the closest any of the new parts have come to their RCP. The buzz around Intel's latest CPUs has been positive enough that demand may have outstripped supply, and so the high-end Core Ultra 7 270K Plus (which is faster than the Core Ultra 9 285K in a lot of situations) is already out of stock in some stores, while the Core Ultra 5 250K Plus is going for some $20 over its suggested price.Intel Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus is $199.99Intel Core Ultra 5 250K Plus is $215So it goes that we have the Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus, which is identical to the 250K, just without the integrated GPU. That's a very slight bummer even for those intending to use powerful discrete gaming GPUs, as it can be handy for troubleshooting (or allowing you to continue using your PC when that big GPU melts its power connector and needs an RMA), but ultimately, the integrated GPU is just inactive silicon in most PC gamers' rigs.AMD does hold the gaming crown in some price bands, but the truth is, there are plenty of PC gamers out there who are all-around power users who make use of their PCs for a lot more than playing video games. You can buy a Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus for $199 and enjoy the multitasking horsepower of eighteen CPU cores cranking at 5.3 GHz, or for the same price, you can pick up a Ryzen 5 9600X, with a whopping six cores. The comparison isn't even salient for multi-core workloads and multi-tasking usage, and in fact, the Intel chip usually wins in gaming against the 9600X anyway.That's right: AMD's Ryzen processors with 3D V-Cache are dominant in gaming workloads, and the Core Ultra 200S Plus series doesn't change that. However, 3D V-Cache processors basically start at $350 with the Ryzen 7 7800X3D outside of some Micro Center doorbusters. That's a brilliant processor, don't get me wrong, and the gaming performance is still a full step ahead of the Intel parts. If you're not gaming in a low resolution (like 1080p) with something like a GeForce RTX 4080 or Radeon RX 9070 XT, though, you'll likely never notice the difference anyway—certainly not the way you'll notice 65% faster render times in HandBrake or 57% faster code compiles.Image 1 of 47(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)(Image credit: Tom's Hardware)There are a few caveats with the newer chips; you'll want the fastest RAM you can afford to throw at the chip (ideally 7200 MT/s or better, although anything will work), and you'll also need to install Intel's Platform Performance Package to get the Application Performance Optimization and Binary Optimization Tool. Fortunately, Intel has made that latter part a one-step process with an all-in-one package instead of requiring users to separately install packages for DTT, PTT, APO, and various other three-letter acronyms.We can't imagine the new Arrow Lake Refresh processors are any less expensive for Intel to fabricate and package, so the company is likely taking a bath on its margins to move these chips. That doesn't matter much to the end-users, though; thanks to Intel's generosity (or desperation), you can pick up a seriously potent 18-core processor for just two-hundred bucks. It's a whole different world from just a few years ago, when Intel's top-end CPU for $539 had a measly eight cores while AMD was shipping 16-core behemoths.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsFollow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
history.author: Zak Killian
history.titleZh: 英特尔劲爆18核酷睿Ultra 5 250KF Plus处理器零售上市，售价不到200美元
history.descriptionZh:
英特尔近期推出的Arrow Lake Refresh处理器系列中，两款新型号——Core Ultra 5 250K Plus和Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus——已正式上市，定价分别为215美元和199美元。此次发布并非因为初代Arrow Lake架构存在显著缺陷，而是英特尔通过价格调整与规格优化，进一步提升了产品的市场竞争力。其中，无集成显卡的250KF Plus版本以199美元的亲民价格，提供了惊人的18核CPU配置，最高频率达5.3 GHz，成为多任务处理与内容创作场景下的高性价比选择。

从技术层面看，Arrow Lake Refresh“Plus”系列主要在原有架构基础上进行了频率优化与软件调校。用户需搭配高速内存（理想速度为7200 MT/s或更高）并安装英特尔统一发布的“平台性能包”，该套件整合了应用性能优化（APO）和二进制优化工具（BOT）等组件，简化了此前需要分步安装多个独立驱动的流程，从而充分发挥处理器性能。

性能对比数据显示，在同价位段中，英特尔Core Ultra 5 250KF Plus（18核）与AMD Ryzen 5 9600X（6核）形成鲜明对比。在多核工作负载如视频渲染（HandBrake）和代码编译中，英特尔处理器领先幅度高达65%和57%。即使在游戏场景中，250KF Plus也普遍优于9600X，尽管AMD的3D V-Cache技术型号（如Ryzen 7 7800X3D）仍在高帧率低分辨率游戏中保持优势，但这些高端型号起价达350美元，与英特尔200美元价位产品不构成直接竞争。

值得注意的是，当前市场对Core Ultra 7 270K Plus等高端型号需求旺盛，部分渠道已出现缺货，而Core Ultra 5 250K Plus的实际售价也略高于184美元的官方建议价。分析认为，英特尔可能为抢占市场份额而主动压缩利润空间，以极具侵略性的定价策略应对AMD在主流市场的竞争。

对于不同用户群体，该系列处理器的价值定位清晰：追求极致游戏帧数且预算充足的玩家仍可能倾向AMD 3D V-Cache产品；但需要兼顾游戏、流媒体、创作等多线程任务的综合型用户，Arrow Lake Refresh处理器凭借翻倍的核心数量与显著的多核性能优势，成为更均衡的选择。无核显的KF版本虽损失了集成显卡的故障排查便利性，但对配备独立显卡的游戏玩家而言，这反而是降低成本的合理选择。

此次价格调整折射出CPU市场竞争的白热化。仅数年前，英特尔539美元的高端产品仅提供8核配置，而如今200美元价位即可获得18核处理器，标志着主流桌面计算平台正式步入“核心战争”新阶段。尽管这可能压缩英特尔短期利润，但最终受益的将是广大消费者，他们能以更低成本获得以往需要高昂投入才能获取的多线程性能。
